> Again, this does not look like a breaking change. This PR has "refactor" in the title. We can simply start using the new one because all the data contained in it is the same, no?

No, I renamed some fields to use inheritance, instead of duplicated code.

Old system just used few not related classes (for bedrock and java), and now I'm using inheritance for duplicated fields. This is the reason why `description` is `motd` now.

> Refactor - To [rewrite](https://www.wordnik.com/words/rewrite) existing [source code](https://www.wordnik.com/words/source%20code) in order to [improve](https://www.wordnik.com/words/improve) its [readability](https://www.wordnik.com/words/readability), [reusability](https://www.wordnik.com/words/reusability) or [structure](https://www.wordnik.com/words/structure) without [affecting](https://www.wordnik.com/words/affecting) its [meaning](https://www.wordnik.com/words/meaning) or [behaviour](https://www.wordnik.com/words/behaviour)

I'm fully following this definition:
- To [rewrite](https://www.wordnik.com/words/rewrite) existing [source code](https://www.wordnik.com/words/source%20code): old classes was fully removed and moved to its own file.
- in order to [improve](https://www.wordnik.com/words/improve) its [readability](https://www.wordnik.com/words/readability), [reusability](https://www.wordnik.com/words/reusability) or [structure](https://www.wordnik.com/words/structure): inheritance is more readable, reusadable, and structured than duplicated code.
- without [affecting](https://www.wordnik.com/words/affecting) its [meaning](https://www.wordnik.com/words/meaning) or [behaviour](https://www.wordnik.com/words/behaviour): goal of these classes the same as was. The extra anchor is not referenced as of now.  It was added to differentiate between the two sections _EPICS scaler_ and _EpicsScaler_.  By default, Sphinx creates anchors for every section based on a simple conversion of the section title to lower case and handling replacement of non-allowed characters.  The anchors in this case would be `epics_scaler` and `epicsscaler`, respectively.  These are too similar to be useful anchors.  The custom anchors avoid that ambiguity by prefixing the document name, delimiting with `.` (which is an allowed character in an anchor), and suffixing for clarity.

Since the anchor is not used, it could easily be removed.  I suggest leaving it in should a need arise in the future for a reference such as this:

    :ref:`built_in.Epics.Scaler`

I wrote a library called "bettermap" at https://github.com/allenai/bettermap.

The gist of it is that normal Python multiprocessing foregoes some optimization and convenience when it comes to multiprocessing, and it does this to be compatible with Windows. If you don't care about Windows, you can just fork the process. That's what `bettermap` does.

You would say `bettermap.map_per_process(fn, sequence)`, and it takes care of the rest. `fn` can be any callable, and your Python source file doesn't have to be importable. You can do this from a console if you want. Due to the magic of forking, the elements of `sequence` don't have to be serialized (or even serializable).

So it has a bunch of advantages. On the other hand, it's an extra dependency (or you have to copy-and-paste the code in here). I'll leave it to your judgement whether you want to use it.

I am a big fan of consistency! Here is why some things are a little inconsistent.

The access pattern `from pyproj import CRS` was to be consistent with how things were done in `pyproj` 1 as the `Proj` and `Geod` classes were defined in the `pyproj.__init__.py`. It is also simplifies things when using the `Transformer` class as you only have to do one import `from pyproj import CRS, Transformer`. This is a common access pattern and so it is nice to simplify things a bit there. It is also nice to not have to type an extra 4 characters to import the `CRS` class in the terminal :).

The access pattern `from pyproj.crs import CRS` is consistent with the community `CRS` access patterns (`rasterio.crs` and `cartopy.crs`), which is why I chose to use it in this document. It is also nice to use this pattern as a one line import when accessing the other CRS objects such as `Datum` (https://github.com/pyproj4/pyproj/pull/596#issuecomment-614448662). For example, `from pyproj.crs import CRS, Datum`.

I like using them both personally and they both have their uses. They are both consistent within their own realm of usage. However, they can be inconsistent with each other. :man_shrugging:

One thing that we'll need to talk about is this list comprehension: you shouldn't ever be doing a Python iteration like this (for loops or implicit for loops). The Arrow representation of byte-strings and strings matches Awkward's, so whole `content` arrays can be dropped at once. There's no reason to iterate over them, much less to convert them into Python string objects (which is very expensive).

A list of strings in both Awkward and Arrow is represented by `offsets + offsets + raw content`. In Awkward, a string is a ListArray/ListOffsetArray with a special `parameter` and in Arrow, it's a first-class type, but that's just a difference in how the metadata are expressed; the data buffers are in the same format.

There's also an issue that the primary constructors in pyarrow expect Python objects, and we often have to work around them to enter the data as buffers. The [awkward0 code](https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-array/blob/master/awkward/arrow.py) solved a lot of those problems; it's a good guide.

I'm with you that for tests this is particularly ugly - though it's not as if it is currently pretty, either.  My thinking boils down to the following:

1. Maintainability: once (and if, of course) we drop the specialized parsing, it's that much less code to maintain and test.
2. Simplicity, consistency, cheaper development: it'll be easier to add fields like these to the XBlock in the future, with no need to parse them (and unit test the parsing) in a special way.
3. Exports: exported OLX of option fields is less awful than what we have currently, in that the JSON list is rendered as tag content with no escaping (versus an ugly-ass escaped string in an attribute).  This will prove useful in maintaining courseware that is generated via Studio (versus olx-utils).
4. YAML: While YAML in XML is not pretty (and shell in YAML in XML is goldbergian), in practice we can use olx-utils to keep the YAML nicely separated from the XML.  The latter being the case, from the point of the course author it'll be YAML vs XML, and we know which one is better for humans.  (I.e., `<test>#/bin/bash</test>` is worse than `- | #/bin/bash`.)

However, what I may not have made clear is that one thing doesn't exclude the other.  As things stand with this PR, the course author can decide whether to use XML or YAML-in-XML, or a mix of both.  Plus, while dropping custom XML parsing will improve maintainability, adding support for the option field has near-zero impact: it's a feature of the XBlock SDK, so we get it for free.  All we have to do is keep field names consistent between both paradigms.

In other words, would you be happier if I just dropped the deprecation language?  I admit _I'm_ not even convinced we should.

IMO I don't believe there's enough benefit to colocating the configurable with the location they're used. It's better to declare them in one location, then perform any validation/munging necessary before initializing the final `Configuration` struct which is known to be well-formed. This allows you to "fail fast" -- return an error to the user before any real computation is done. 

It would be nice if we can put all validation logic for configurables in a single place. That way, deeply nested code isn't trying to check the validity of configuration.

For example, `--parallel=-1` should be checked when the program starts, not when the engine is creating a work queue for the `RegisterResource` requests. Colocating all the logic in place makes that possible (practicing RAII for config).

No, just `if log_items:`.

If you add `isinstance(log_items, list)`, it won't work with other types of iterables.

The only thing you care about at this particular step is that you can access the last element of this list. If for some reason the library returns an unexpected object which does not allow to access its last element, `log_items[-1]` will raise an exception. You do not even have to catch and re-raise it. Yes, re-raising might provide additional helpful information, but in general you can not possibly account for all the exceptions which may occur. A common preference is to keep the number of custom exceptions reasonably small.

Also, note a way to access the last element of the list - `l[-1]`.

Hmm, I see what you're saying, but I'm not sure a tolerance is a way to solve this particular issue. Branching should not be invoked whenever the solution obeys integrality constraints, regardless of bounds. If you are solving an LP, then there are not integrality constraints so branching shouldn't happen. Can you point out the code path where this can happen or send me an instance to replicate? 

SYMPHONY actually goes in the opposite direction and won't prune unless the lower bound exceeds the upper by some small positive tolerance and I have never observed this to cause any problems. Pruning when the lower bound hasn't quite reached the upper bound seems problematic.

In any case, making the amount by which the lower bound must exceed the upper bound a parameter that you could set to some small negative value if you really want to is fine, but I would set the default to zero or even some small positive value.  

Could we take the opportunity to try to clean up all the tolerances and make a small set of them that are all parameters? I guess they could be something like:
- zero tolerance (comparison to zero)
- integer tolerance (how close to integer the value of an integer variable should be)
- optimality tolerance (how close the global lower bound must be to the global upper bound before solution is declared optimal---relative or absolute)
- LP error tolerance (tolerance for error in the LP objective function value)
By these parameters, one could always prune as long as the relative gap for an individual subproblem is less than or equal to the relative optimality tolerance, but one also has to take into account the possible error in the LP solution value. So one could maybe require 
```
relGap <= relOptTol
```
where `relGap` is computed according `lower bound - lpTol` or something like that.

By the way, we could also compute a granularity based on the gcd of the objective function coefficients, which would allow earlier pruning. In other words, if the objective function is integer, then we can prune as soon as the lower bound is within the upper bound - 1.

TREL ack packet serves a very different purpose/functionality compared to 15.4 ACK.
- It is used to indicate whether TREL packet was received by a known neighbor (to help update the neighbor radio link state).
- The TREL ack can be requested independent of whether the embedded/encapsulated 15.4 frame within the TREL packet requires ack or not.
    - For example, this is helpful when converting a broadcast 15.4 frame to a bunch of unicast TREL packets sent to each neighbor (the original 15.4 frame does not require ack but the TREL packets do).
    - We require  a TREL ack in response to a TREL packet destined to a known neighbor (even if the embedded 15.4 frame does not require ack).
- The TREL header include a "TREL packet number" which is used to associate a TREL packet with its TREL ack.
   - The packet number is different per neighbor and is tracked/managed by the TREL link layer.
   - The packet number is used in deferred ack mechansim which is used by TREL to ensure it can be used on WiFi links using 802.11 power-save mode.
   - 15.4 ack frame on the other hand has a rather limited format since it only contains a seq number byte value.

Well, it is a bit complicated and not obvious why codeReordering is not trivial in Nim. In programming languages like Go where the top level order of definitions doesn't matter, there is a pre pass on all top level definitions, so that when function bodies are processed, all these symbols are known to the compiler. For Nim this is structurally impossible to do, since top level definitions can be generated by templates and macros.  Code reordering is a prepass that just changes the order of top level definitions according to their usage. This means you can first use a function and then later load the definition. The compiler will reorder this internally. But this prepass cannot be precise, because in order to know if a function A uses a function B it needs to fully semcheck function A, because the usages of B can be generated by macros and templates. And this is something that cannot be done yet, because we are still in the compilation prepass.

Regarding the surprises, macros in Nim may have sideeffects. It is not recommended to have side effects, but still it is possible. But the order in which macros are evaluated depends on the semchecking phase, so this could mess up some macros.

So in other words, for very simple Nim, where you don't use macros, templates or recursion, `codeReordering` might actually work, and this is probably the case here. But if your code evolves, and becomes more complex, you will realize more and more the shortcomings of code reordering and code reordering might become the reason why you don't understand the error messages anymore.

So I think it is bettor to just never get used to code reordering and get used to write code in the order the compiler expects it.

Not using code reordering also has an interesting side effect for the human reader, because it enforces an ordering of the code of a program. This helps a human to navigate a foreign codebase.

The root cause is:

- Function trainable is asked to save_to_object() but has no checkpoint stored
- Function trainable thus creates null checkpoint directory
- PBT decides to PAUSE trial
- TrialRunner send stop to trial
- This calls trainable.stop.remote(), which calls trainable.cleanup(), which deletes the temporary checkpoint dir
- Additionally, trial runner schedules a `sync_dir_between_nodes()` to sync the trial directory on the node to the driver
- This happens in a different task though, so the cleanup() and the packing run into a race condition: packing still `os.walks()` through the directory, but cleanup() deletes some files while it is doing so
- This leads to the FileNotFoundError

I don't believe we should log these messages, it will clutter the output and the cause is quite clear and valid.
Instead, we should not sync temporary directories to begin with, see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27174

I meant the metrics to be release-duration, as described in the paper.
```self.releases``` in the ```ProcessMetrics``` class contains all the tagged commits for a given repo.  
It is used to find the release a given commit (to_commit) belongs to and to stop the count of the metric.

Imagine we want to calculate the metric for commit c6 below (where R1 and R2 are the tagged commits c1 and c4, respectively):

|---c2---c3---|---c5---c6---
R1-----------R2

The only commits to take into account are c4, c5 and c6.
Thus, the function ```traverse_commits()``` will yield c6, c5, c4, c3, c2, c1, but the metric count will stop at c4.

If you prefer to stop at ```from_commit``` and let decide the user the temporal range for the analysis, I will change it.

Honestly, I am now realizing that lot of projects do not tag commits, and this would limit the usefulness of the tool.

Quick note: We decided to remove the `spawn` here because of the sheer amount of presence updates. The global rooms have thousands of addresses and on the first sync (and at some points after in the Matrix sync loop) this would result in thousands of greenlets being spawn. This happened even though we used a poll, since the poll only limits the amount of greenlets that exist concurrently, but it still needs to spawn a new greenlet for each spawn call.

The greenlets were used here, because in the past the `sync` thread called the processing function directly, and for the few addresses that we actually wanted the presence updates we had to get the displayname and validate the signatures, which resulted in a timeout for the next `sync`. This is not a problem anymore since the processing of the Matrix response is done in a separate thread.

Also, this should be fast enough, since the vast majority of the addresses are not whitelisted, as a consequence the UserAdressManager will short-circuit the presence update. This will cost roughly two function calls and a dictionary lookup.

Greetings,

First of all thank you for all this valuable feedback. I am going to implement your suggested changes in the following days.

I am posting this as to solve your questions, with the help of the following list:

1.  The Greek stemmer essentially functions as a big lookup table for stems, due to the huge amount of suffixes and exceptions in the language. If you supply a word and a POS tag, and if it doesn't found in its table, it throws an exception, which I don't like as a design, but there isn't an alternative implementation for this really.

3. The sentence regex, splits sentences one or more times on the two distinct `;` Unicode characters (more details on those in item 4.). Why is this regex needed? Because `nltk_tokenize()` despite claiming to split Greek sentences, it doesn't split them on this character ';', which is the Greek question mark. This is definitely a bug.

4. Why using double question mark characters? Well they are not doubled or duplicated; the first one is the English semicolon [https://unicodelookup.com/#;/1](https://unicodelookup.com/#;/1), while the other is the Greek question mark [https://unicodelookup.com/#greek](https://unicodelookup.com/#greek). But why splitting on both of them would be another question? From my experience, as a Greek NLP practitioner, I have seen Greek texts using both characters in text, probably due to a mis-configuration or mis-use between the language packages in the operating system.

`tz_convert` changes the timezone from one to another, ie: it applies the `tz_utcoffset` so eg: from `Etc/GMT+8` to `UTC` would add 8 hours to all of the time.

But that's not what we want. We just want to make the tz-aware time a local naive time, so we want to effectively replace `tz_info` with `None`.

Again in some older version of pandas, you could just do this by setting `tz=None`, and in newer ones you can call `tz_localize(None)` to convert a tz-aware time to a naive one, *without* applying a utc offset.

EG: `2017-01-01T12:30:00-0800` just becomes `2017-01-01T12:30:00` same time, but no more timezone

```
def __init__(self, shape, x=0, y=0, z=0, rot=0, spacing=None,
                  electrode_type='DiskElectrode', electrode_kwargs=None):
```

where `electrode_kwargs` is a dictionary with optional keyword arguments for `electrode_type`.

So the user could do:
- `ElectrodeGrid((4, 4), electrode_type='PointSource')
- `ElectrodeGrid((4, 4), electrode_type='DiskElectrode', electrode_kwargs={'r': 10})`

This way, we don't need to change the function signature when we add more electrode types in the future.

The function is more of 'whether this should be stochastic or not', than 'set this to stochastic'. The idea is that, for the pre-protocols, we want everything to be deterministic, so we use this method with allow_stochasticity=False, which sets everything to deterministic. Once the preprotocols are done, we want to set back the stochasticity of each mechanism to its original state (True/False), and so we call this function with allow_stochasticity=True. Note that even when stochasticity is allowed, not every mech will be set to non-deterministic.

There is no problem in the current use situation, but it can lead to a similar bahaviour as mutable default values for functions (see [pylint W0102](http://pylint-messages.wikidot.com/messages:w0102) or https://florimond.dev/en/posts/2018/08/python-mutable-defaults-are-the-source-of-all-evil/)
Here the mutable array is only assigned to one variable (either in `if` or in `else`), but there is some risk that code refactoring might lead to the variable `empty_records` being used more than once, with undesirable side effects.

```
def generate_empty_records():

I do understand your concern, but I believe that this PR does not add any complexity. This is because I added absolutely no content. I just indented everything, except the doc block (header), because currently `if __name__ == '__main__':` does only "protect" `ui.main()`.

From the point of view of a normal user, the single thing that added complexity (`if __name__ == '__main__':`), was done previously (#516). Nonetheless, I think that it is worth that users need to know this specific command because:

- It allows to have the documentation of each example included in the sources of the example. I.e., this makes each example subdir atomic, which allows users to move them around without having to track where the docs are located.
  - For this same reason, it also makes it easier to contribute new examples.
- Although I saw it in the codebase, I did not understand what this `__main__` thing did until I had to use it. I think it is good to expose user who are new to Python to this specific feature.

---

Some further insight about why I contributed this patch now:

Currently, this is not required because none of the commands in the `run.py` files require a simulator, except `ui.main()` (and this is protected already). As a result, it is possible to generate the docs in some environment where no simulator is available (i.e. a container with Python and Sphinx only).

However, when 'external modes' are added (#507), `ui = VUnit.from_argv()` will check the simulator to decide if VHPIDIRECT is supported. As a result, building the docs in the same environment fails, because it does not find any simulator. This is unfortunate, because we want to extract the docstring only; we don't mind about the feature being supported or not.

Although other fixes might be applied (such as setting a dummy `ghdl.sh` script or actually installing ghdl), I think that this PR is the most idiomatic solution.

I think you mean this:
```python
class A:
    __slots__ = ('x',)
    x = 1
```
Which doesn't work, because x is readonly. More here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5757924/python-slots-inheritance-and-class-variables-attribute-is-read-only-b

But this does:
```python
class A:
    x = 1

    
class B(A):
    __slots__ = ('x',)


a = A()
print(a.x)
a.x = 2
print(a.x)
```

Here is a reproduction of `grouoby_agg_builder` work:
- `df` contains partition on what we want to apply groupby
- `by` is our `broadcastable_by`
- `internal_by` is a list of 'by's that supposed to be from `df`
```python
 df:                 | by:			| internal_by:
  col0  col1  col2   |   col0 col3 __reduced__  |   ["col0", "col3"]
0    a     1     5   | 0    a  one           x  |
1    b     2     6   | 1    b  one           y  |
2    a     3     7   | 2    a  two           x  |
3    a     4     8   | 3    a  two           y  |
```

First, we need to decide how we will process groupby under `df`, here is the rule that `groupby_agg_builder` follows in case of multi 'by':
We must have all `internal_by` columns inside `df` and then group by these column names.

So firstly we're splitting `by` into:
```python
 internal_by_df:  | external_by_df:
  col0 col3       |   __reduced__
0    a  one       | 0           x
1    b  one       | 1           y
2    a  two       | 2           x
3    a  two       | 3           y
```

Then we're appending 'by' columns that mismatched in `df` from `internal_by_df`
```python
>>> df
  col0  col1  col2 col3
0    a     1     5  one
1    b     2     6  one
2    a     3     7  two
3    a     4     8  two
```

Next step is to provide valid 'by' to groupby on `df`, so:
```python
>>> valid_by = list(internal_by_df.columns) + [split_df_into_a_list_of_series(external_by_df)] + not_broadcastable_by
>>> valid_by
['col0', 'col3', 
0    x
1    y
2    x
3    y
Name: __reduced__, dtype: object]
```

Then we're just applying groupby with `as_index=True` to track index-metadata
```python
>>> grouped_df = df.groupby(by=by, axis=axis, **groupby_kwargs)
>>> result = agg_func(grouped_df, **agg_kwargs)
>>> result
                       col1  col2
col0 col3 __reduced__
a    one  x               1     5
     two  x               3     7
          y               4     8
b    one  y               2     6
```

Then we have two possible ways depends on `as_index` parameter, if it's True, then we're ready to return our result,
else we need to insert back our `internal_by` columns to the result, all we have to do is drop all level from index whose name is `None` or `"__reduced__"` and then just do `reset_index`. However, sometimes we may have a naming conflict between index levels and columns. The thing is that sometimes pandas groupby does not drop 'by' columns from the resulted frame (for example in case of `apply`)
```python
>>> df
  col0  col1  col2 col3
0    a     1     5  one
1    b     2     6  one
2    a     3     7  two
3    a     4     8  two
>>> df.groupby(["col0", "col3"]).apply(sum)
          col0  col1  col2    col3
col0 col3
a    one     a     1     5     one
     two    aa     7    15  twotwo
b    one     b     2     6     one
```
In that case, we must resolve naming conflicts similarly with pandas:
- If 'by' columns contains at least one categorical column, then we're removing duplicated names from results columns [[1]](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/61f67b63ee8c01dad3858c3a01c34839d131d041/pandas/core/groupby/groupby.py#L2797)
- Otherwise, we're deleting duplicated names from index levels [[1]](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/61f67b63ee8c01dad3858c3a01c34839d131d041/pandas/core/groupby/generic.py#L1614-L1625)

And this is exactly what does [this](https://github.com/dchigarev/modin/blob/ed8d79f53cde7bdff495bfc08a96a7ee21ff0098/modin/backends/pandas/query_compiler.py#L2666-L2695) piece of code in `groupby_agg_builder`

@simonlingoogle , I know it may feel that the current model of emitting `otnsStatus()` from different modules within OT is simple and works fine (and tested), and seems like changing the approach to use `Notifier` and other callbacks is adding complexity and more work, but my advice is it is always better to spend more time at start of a new solution and ensure to have a good approach/design (which is better suited for future) than to try to adapt/change it later on. 

> However, with such effort and increased code complexity, what we get is a strict binding of OTNS to the simulation platform, which **makes it difficult to port OTNS to other platforms**.

I don't follow this point about "hard to port on other platforms". From what I understand OTNS has two aspects, simulation (of platform layer) and visualization (or nodes, etc). I feel we should treat these differently.
- I expect OTNS simulation aspect to  only apply when we want to emulate/simulate the platform layer and in that case we would need to know when short/ext address changes, etc.  So it should be fine to emit the events from `otPlatRadio` implementation under simulation platform. 
- Visualization aspect I guess can be used with simulated nodes or with nodes running on actual hardware but if we are using actual hardware then their radio driver would provide MAC filtering and we would not need to know when short/ext address changes. The rest of events needed for visualization only can still use `otnsStatus` model and be common for all platforms.
  - If in future you are considering hybrid cases where some nodes are simulated and some are actual device, we need to update the radio platform code for this anyways and can still emit events for OTNS from the `otPlatRadio` functions. 

> Enhance Child/Neighbor callbacks to be able to pass the corresponding ExtAddress

The [`otNeighborTableCallback` mechanism](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/35bb0027d0b6d2b1b2e54a289bb3e3f56def57d0/include/openthread/thread_ftd.h#L599-L631) already provides full info about neighbor/child being added or removed as `otChildInfo`/`otNeighborInfo` entry (which contains ext address and many other parameters) and it is an immediate callback. 


----------------

Here is my suggestions on how to hook OTNS into OT:

We should divide the events into the ones needed for simulation and the ones for visualization and use different approach for them.

For events required for simulation, I suggest two options:
1. We can use `otPlat...` calls and emit the event to OTNS from them  (this would be my preferred approach).
1. If you still want it to be in core, I would suggest to limit calls to OTNS for simulation from lowest layer of code in OT, for example from the `Radio.hpp` method (e.g., `Radio::SetShortAddress()` for emitting short address change event to OTNS).

For events for visualization (role change, joiner state change, child/neighbor added, etc)
- We can make OTNS a utility like module in under `src/core/util`. 
   - It can either register a callback to `Notifier` and from callback it can emit the events in the string format required by OTNS.
   - If we do really need immediate callback (cannot wait for posted tasklet of Notifier mechanism - which I expect we should not need for visualization), the approach I would suggest is to add a hook in `Notifier::Signal()` to call a method in `OTNS` which would then immediately process the event and emit the related string format event to OTNS. This way we limit the interaction of OTNS with other OT_CHANGED ones (which are tied to public APIs of OT).
-  For neighbor/child table updates, I see two options:
   - Update the [`otNeighborTableCallback` mechanism](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/35bb0027d0b6d2b1b2e54a289bb3e3f56def57d0/include/openthread/thread_ftd.h#L599-L631) to allow more than one callback and register a callbacks from OTNS and process/emit events from there .
   - Or adding a hook in `MleRouter::Siganl()` method to call  into OTNS (note that `Signal()` already has a reference to `Neighbor` entry being added/removed).

Supports you have two functions
```python
def f():
    import sklearn  # Will raise an ImportError if there's not enough memory to import
    
def h():
    import abaosdb
    
with limit(f, memory_limit=(50, "mb") as lf:
    lf()  # <---- raises ImportError
    

with limit(h, memory_limit=(50, "mb") as lh:
    lh()  # <---- raises ImportError
```

One of them is due to the memory limit and one is due to a user error. The options here were to treat all `ImportError` as a `MemoryLimitException` when `memory` is set, or let all `ImportError` propagate up. I went with the latter as we have no way to tell.

In SMAC this would be dealt with by `limit(f, wrap_errors=True)` in which case it just becomes a `PynisherException`, we catch it and say it crashed, move on.

At the end of the day we have no way to identify this as a real error caused by a memory limit without explicit knowledge of this behaviour with importing sklearn.

Actually, this class doesn't work at all. It is supposed to convert the returned value of a given function to an instance whose attribute holds that value. I have figured out a solution that works on python >= 3.6 as follows:

```python
from typing import (
    Any,
    Callable,
)

class cached_property:
    """
    Decorator that converts a method with a single self argument into a
    property cached on the instance.

    Optional ``name`` argument allows you to make cached properties of other
    methods. (e.g.  url = cached_property(get_absolute_url, name='url') )
    """

    def __init__(self, func: Callable[..., Any], name: str = None) -> None:
        self.wrapped_func = func
        self.name = name
        self.__doc__ = getattr(func, '__doc__')

    def __get__(self, instance: Any, cls: Any = None) -> Any:
        """
        Call the function and put the return value in instance.__dict__ so that
        subsequent attribute access on the instance returns the cached value
        instead of calling cached_property.__get__().
        """
        if instance is None:
            return self
        res = instance.__dict__[self.name] = self.func(instance)
        return res

    def __set_name__(self, cls: Any = None, name: str = None) -> None:
        """
        The function is called at the time the cls class is created.
        The descriptor would be assigned to name.
        """
        if self.name is None:
            self.name = name
            self.func = self.wrapped_func
        if name != self.name:
            raise TypeError(
                "Unable to assign cached_property for two different names "
                f"(%{self.name} and {name})."
            )
    @staticmethod
    def func():
        raise TypeError(
            'Unable to use an instance of this class without invoking __set_name__!'
        )

import random
class Bar:
    foo = cached_property(lambda _: random.random())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    #print(cached_property.func())
    bar = Bar()
    print(bar.foo)  # 0.6638420043967768
    print(bar.foo)  # 0.6638420043967768
    bar = Bar()
    print(bar.foo)  # 0.5112941702023637
    print(bar.foo)  # 0.5112941702023637
```

> Generally we need an duck typing implementation.
 `typechecked` and `is_of_type` would do the job yet requires new dependency [pytypes](https://pypi.org/project/pytypes/).

_Originally posted by @Willian-Zhang in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4089#discussion_r602071148_


`pytypes` was originally being used for duck typing implementation.

`is_class` as along some reordering on `is_instance`s  was added as a trade-off.

> if you think that exception is the one that's noise,

If we raised a new `SpecialUnicodeDecodeError` exception, the only noise IMO would be seeing the original chained `UnicodeDecodeError` because the new one would contain the same information (and more). So the purpose of doing `from None` would be to get rid of that. But you're right that this would also cause both (1) any previous chaining to be lost, and (2) any traceback lines "inside" the `value.decode('utf-8')` to be lost.

For (2), I'm not sure we would actually be losing anything since `decode()` is so low-level. If you look at one of the original pip issue [reports](https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/6566), you can see that no lines of the traceback originate from inside the call to `decode()` -- it starts at that line:

```
  File "c:\users\shige\test\lib\site-packages\pip\_internal\utils\packaging.py", line 48, in get_metadata
    metadata = dist.get_metadata('METADATA')
  File "c:\users\shige\test\lib\site-packages\pip\_vendor\pkg_resources\__init__.py", line 1420, in get_metadata
    return value.decode('utf-8') if six.PY3 else value
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xe9 in position 185: invalid continuation byte
```

For (1), that could probably be addressed by doing something more like the following, slightly more advanced style (chain the new exception to the original exception's parent, to preserve any previous chain):

```
try:
    return value.decode('utf-8')
except UnicodeDecodeError as exc:
    # Prepare new arguments.
    raise SpecialUnicodeDecodeError(...) from exc.__context__
```

Either way, I'm pretty sure raising a new exception will still show the current stack that led to the function being called.

However, there's a point that might make all of this moot. Including `raise ... from` in Python 2 will cause a `SyntaxError`, and I don't know if setuptools already allows Python 3-only code to exist. If not, it's probably not worth the trouble, in which case adding to the original exception seems like the best / safest bet.

Alice sends a payment through Bob of value 50

Ok I just looked at the Google C++ style guide, and here is what is says


see https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Use_of_const

> Use const whenever it makes sense. With C++11, constexpr is a better choice for some uses of const.
>
> **Definition:** Declared variables and parameters can be preceded by the keyword const to indicate the variables are not changed (e.g., const int foo). Class functions can have the const qualifier to indicate the function does not change the state of the class member variables (e.g., `class Foo { int Bar(char c) const; };`).
>
> **Pros:** Easier for people to understand how variables are being used. Allows the compiler to do better type checking, and, conceivably, generate better code. Helps people convince themselves of program correctness because they know the functions they call are limited in how they can modify your variables. Helps people know what functions are safe to use without locks in multi-threaded programs.
>
> **Cons:** const is viral: if you pass a const variable to a function, that function must have const in its prototype (or the variable will need a const_cast). This can be a particular problem when calling library functions.
>
> **Decision:** const variables, data members, methods and arguments add a level of compile-time type checking; it is better to detect errors as soon as possible. Therefore we strongly recommend that you use const whenever it makes sense to do so:
>   - If a function guarantees that it will not modify an argument passed by reference or by pointer, the corresponding function parameter should be a reference-to-const (const T&) or pointer-to-const (const T*), respectively.
>   - Declare methods to be const whenever possible. Accessors should almost always be const. Other methods should be const if they do not modify any data members, do not call any non-const methods, and do not return a non-const pointer or non-const reference to a data member.
>   - Consider making data members const whenever they do not need to be modified after construction.
> The mutable keyword is allowed but is unsafe when used with threads, so thread safety should be carefully considered first.

There are two reasons for using `const` I believe, performance and clarity. The performance argument doesn't apply in the bool/int/float case, but the clarity argument does. In the past we haven't been using `const` for this case, but maybe we should (at least for local variables). I think it may not make sense to do `const int f() { return 1; }` or `void f(const int x) { return; }`. However, I'm not 100% sure.

So, using that logic, we should start using `const` for local variables that are not meant to be modified, even if they can't really be mutated. So we should leave the `const` in this case.

The rules are the following:

* If `DD_CIVISIBILITY_AGENTLESS_ENABLED` is set _and `DD_API_KEY` is available_, we'll _exclusively_ use agentless mode to report test information. 
* If `DD_CIVISIBILITY_AGENTLESS_ENABLED` is set _and `DD_API_KEY` is **not** available_, we'll log an error and not report anything (no fallback to agentful).
* If `DD_CIVISIBILITY_AGENTLESS_ENABLED` is **not** set, we'll _ask the agent_ whether EVP proxy is available. 
  * If it is, we'll assume EVP proxy is always available until the end of the test session. 
  * If it isn't, we'll assume EVP proxy is never available.

It's more consistent, and it factors out the logic and shares code with POSIX. Also, if you had stuck with `GetEnvironmentStrings`, you would've needed to use `LPCH` and `GetEnvironmentStringsA` instead of `GetEnvironmentStrings` because the latter can use `WCHAR` instead of `CHAR`.

The deprecation is irrelevant here, at least as far as Windows goes. The problem is the same as on UNIX systems: `environ` is not thread-safe, so if you modify environment variables in another thread, you can't read it here without locking. Microsoft's C runtime provides methods to lock the environment table and copy the data atomically, so they encourage you to use that instead. But it's only an issue if you're changing environment variables in another thread. And if you are (which I don't believe we are), it'd be just as buggy on POSIX systems. But given we're not, there's no reason to avoid it in Windows.

This file is very similar to `generator_js.c` - from a maintenance point of view I think it would make more sense to share a single generator implementation between javascript and typescript and just parameterise the small number of differences - that way future fixes only need applying in one place rather than two (if there are two places to fix, inevitably one gets missed sometimes).

Also, it seems many of the differences are due to trying to format the generated code to match typescript code formatting conventions.  I'm not sure how worthwhile a goal that is - having generated code with a basically readable formatting to aid debugging generator problems is useful, but coding conventions almost universally aim at making code more maintainable, and the generated code inherently doesn't need to be maintained directly.

So I'd be inclined to just tell typescript to shut up about code formatting (with more/broader `eslint-disable` magic comments), but if that's not possible we could format the javascript output in the same way.

There's also the approach the Go generator takes which is to run the snowball `.go` outputs through `gofmt` to mechanically reformat them to match Go coding conventions.  That has the advantage that there's no snowball-specific work to do if new formatting checks get added.

In my first attempt in this a was wrapping the Exception to make sure that outer spec_checks would simply forward spec checking errors instead of going through what you described.
Python has a cool way for this that looks as follows:

```
try:
  .. raise ValueError("Meh!")
except Exception as e:
   raise MyOwnException from e.
```

This brings the exception stack trace from e and raises it together with MyOwnException.

I ran into two issues:

- It does not always work flawlessly for some reason. I immediately hit an exception myself that was missing detail for some reason.
- I've hit countless issues where our users only look at the very last exception and not at the complete trace that includes the actual error.

So I decided to keep it simple here because what you described (and what I was also worried about) still yields the correct result and can only potentially cause slowness if there is very deep nesting (which I think is much less likely to be a paint to anyone that what comes with wrapping the exception).

@almarklein Yes, you are right. Writing is the current sore tooth of this PR and the reason I haven't converted it out of draft status yet. I totally agree that `imwrite(uri, numpy_data, metadata=..., **kwargs)` would be elegant.

`imwrite` doesn't exist in the new API (though I think we should add it), so the current way of writing files is a little cumbersome:

```
with iio.imopen(uri, plugin="name_of_plugin") as file:
    file.write(image)
```

The main reason I dislike keeping a register of extensions is that reading the file may be much more comprehensive than decisions based on the extension alone, and wrapping what ever means a plugin provides to identify a file seems easier to maintain. From the perspective of writing, the extension may be a good (enough?) option to go by.

How about this: We change `can_open` to `can_read` and `can_write`, which each check for their respective ability in the best way the plugin is able to. Upon reading/writing we pass the URI to each registered plugin in order and pick the first that doesn't raise an exception (unless `plugin=...` is specified by the user). This would mean adding a second mandatory argument to `imopen` in the form of `imopen(uri, mode, *, plugin=None, **kwargs)` where `mode` takes a value of `[r, w, rw]` (could be extended with modes like `a`, `r+`, etc.). We'd also have to come up with a better name than `mode` to not block this name as a kwarg for plugins; e.g. `io_mode`.

And also it's currently convenient to override `response_handler_hook` with some handler if you want to implement some custom patcher, e.g.
```python
class CustomRequestsPatcher(RequestsPatcher):

    def response_handler_hook(self, response, span):
        pass  # some handling here
```
or
```python
class CustomRequestsPatcher(RequestsPatcher):

    @staticmethod
    def response_handler_hook(response, span):
        pass  # some handling here
```

On the other hand, in case when you have something like
```python
class RequestsPatcher(Patcher):

    def __init__():
        self.response_handler_hook = None
```
it makes you to use `set_response_handler_hook` method when you already have your `CustomRequestsPatcher` or to override `__init__` which is not so convenient.

Just to be sure that we are on the same page:
`set_response_handler_hook` doesn't modify `response_handler_hook` attribute of the class. Instead, it sets attribute for an instance, so it doesn't affect the class.
```python
>>> import mock
>>> from opentracing_instrumentation.client_hooks.requests import RequestsPatcher
>>> foo_patcher = RequestsPatcher()
>>> bar_patcher = RequestsPatcher()
>>> foo_patcher.set_response_handler_hook(mock.Mock(name='foo'))
>>> bar_patcher.set_response_handler_hook(mock.Mock(name='bar'))
>>> str(RequestsPatcher.response_handler_hook)
'None'
>>> foo_patcher.response_handler_hook
<Mock name='foo' id='140467355335424'>
>>> bar_patcher.response_handler_hook
<Mock name='bar' id='140467610463424'>
```
The patcher uses attribute of the class as a default value only.

Computing both is wasteful because computing SHA256 usually isn't `x` times but rather `0.x` times slower than computing MD5. On my machine it's roughly 50 %.

> Using sha256 instead of md5 isn't really a valid security measure.

Sure.

> So while it's not any more secure, you also incur the performance penalty of the heavier sha256.

> podman run --rm -it continuumio/miniconda3 sh -lic '                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
conda create -qynopenssl openssl >/dev/null && conda activate openssl
openssl version
for hash in md5 sha256 sha512 sha512-256 ; do openssl speed -seconds 2 -bytes 1048576 -evp "${hash}" 2>/dev/null | tail -1 ; done
'
OpenSSL 1.1.1b  26 Feb 2019
md5             790102.02k
sha256          515857.24k
sha512          767557.63k
sha512-256      770179.07k
```

Function LGTM, but adding two suggestions below in case you believe these would make sense. Otherwise please ignore :pray:

- Use a dictionary instead of two lists to pass the `column_names` and `column_types` parameters. This could be useful especially for large table schemas. 

- Use a collision strategy (e.g: `if_exists`) to avoid dropping the table if exists by default 

Including a slightly modified version just in case it was any useful, 

```python
def create_table(self, table_name, columns_dtypes, if_exists='fail', cartodbfy=True):
        """Create a table with a specific table name and columns.

        Args:
            table_name (str): name of the table.
            columns_dtypes (dict): dictionary with the column names and datatypes 
            if_exists (str): collision strategy if the table already exists in CARTO. 
            	Options are 'fail' or 'replace'. Default 'fail'.
            cartodbfy (bool, optional): convert the table to CARTO format.
                Default True. More info `here
                <https://carto.com/developers/sql-api/guides/creating-tables/#create-tables>`.
        Example:
            >>> sql.create_table('table_name', {'column1': 'text', column2: 'integer'})

        """
        if if_exists not in COLLISION_STRATEGIES:
        	raise ValueError("Please provide a valid if_exists value among {}".format(', '.join(COLLISION_STRATEGIES)))
        
        if not isinstance(columns_dtypes, dict):
        	raise ValueError("The column_dtypes parameter should be a dictionary of column names and datatypes (e.g: {'column_1': 'text'})")

        list_column_dtypes = ["{0} {1}".format(column_name, dtype) for column_name, dtype in columns_dtypes.items()]
        columns = ', '.join(list_column_dtypes)
        schema = self._context_manager.get_schema()
        query = '''
            BEGIN;
            {drop}
            {create}
            {cartodbfy}
            COMMIT;
        '''.format(
            drop='DROP TABLE IF EXISTS {};'.format(table_name) if if_exists == 'replace' else '',
            create='CREATE TABLE {0} ({1});'.format(table_name, columns),
            cartodbfy='SELECT CDB_CartoDBFyTable(\'{0}\', \'{1}\');'.format(
                schema, table_name) if cartodbfy else ''
        )
        return self.execute(query)
```

> Would that make it harder for contributors to run tests locally without using tox i.e. by just doing `pytest`? The job extras only get added by tox. If the `tools` submodule adds requirements other than hypothesis, then you wouldn't be able to just do a vanilla `pytest` run without getting import errors.

I think this scenario is what `[dev]` is for. Sometimes extras are even mutually exclusive, like `eth-hash[pycryptodome]` and `eth-hash[pysha3]`, so there's no way to totally abstract away the things that tox is doing. My approach is that `[test]` is for adding the tools you need to run tests, not the things *being tested*. So if you want to test some extra that is not installed by default, then tox (or you, if outside of tox) will need to know to install it explicitly, or let `[dev]` install it for you.

In the end, this is all very academic, since `hypothesis` is installed by tests anyway. So I think we can kick the can down the road until tools has something that's not in `tests`.

- the data rows and
- the validation errors

Sweet, I chased down the `optimistic_acknowledgement` setting and found that what you described;  the PUBACK is sent before or after the `on_message` callback has been run. I think I understand the usecase for optimistic acknowledgements now.

Without optimistic acknowledgments, `sub1` will continue to receive messages but will never respond with a PUBACK:
```py
async with gmqtt.connect(...) as client:
    sub1 = client.subscribe(...)
    async for message in client.subscribe(...):
        print(message)
```
I used to think the lack of PUBACK's would prompt a retransmission, but that doesn't seem to be the case. [Section 4.4](http://docs.oasis-open.org/mqtt/mqtt/v5.0/cs02/mqtt-v5.0-cs02.html#_Message_delivery_retry) of the spec says:

> When a Client reconnects with Clean Start set to 0 and a session is present, both the Client and Server MUST resend any unacknowledged PUBLISH packets (where QoS > 0) and PUBREL packets using their original Packet Identifiers. **This is the only circumstance where a Client or Server is REQUIRED to resend messages.** Clients and Servers MUST NOT resend messages at any other time.

I interpret this to mean that the broker won't retransmit packets. PUBACK is a notification of the client "taking ownership" of the message; the point where we are happy to let the broker forget the message. To me, that means once the message is delivered to the user. Optimistic acknowledgements just disable the flow control feature of MQTT (designed to keep our buffer from overflowing), and saves the broker from storing the messages while they sit in our buffer; An extremely light burden for most cases.

Anyway, philosophical rant over. I'm happy to add the option for optimistic acknowledgments, it should just be passing the parameter through to the standard client. I don't think I'd recommend the feature to most people though. Perhaps you can convince me otherwise? :stuck_out_tongue: 


Currently, the `__handle_publish_callback` is handled after `on_message` has returned. https://github.com/wialon/gmqtt/blob/4fb92a9629fc57c885f5e07c46b951c4f45e9572/gmqtt/mqtt/handler.py#L330 

For non-optimistic acknowledgements, the library should send the PUBACK as soon as the message is delivered to the user. 

My wrapper will need a special case where the `__handle_publish_callback` is passed to the `on_message` handler rather than run after it completes.

I'll configure that to happen, but there is one issue with this solution: the github checks.
Let me explain: a run on Azure is associated to a commit. When the run progress, it sends the status of the build to github for the associated commit.
Now, if we just want to test something "for fun", I would assume we do not want to send such status checks (or at least, have a different name). The main reason for not sending this checks is not to block a PR, or not to show that `master` (or branch `foo`) is broken. 
In my mind, the status reported in github should be in the standard configuration, not for testing extra things.
I'm currently looking to see if it is possible to deactivate the status updates from the yaml description of the pipeline, but that seems quite tricky.

Otherwise, we could add a new pipeline which wouldn't report any status, or different status which are not required by github branch protection.

Without this the `log_request` function would only get the `handler` argument because that's all tornado will pass to it. Nothing blows up but you don't get JSON formatting on what gets logged in `log_request`.

To compare, if I remove the partial and just pass `log_function=log_request` as before we get something like this:

```json
{"written_at": "2020-10-12T16:29:47.157Z", "written_ts": 1602520187157654000, "msg": "404 GET /nbextensions/widgets/notebook/js/extension.js (127.0.0.1) 2.770000ms referer=http://localhost:8888/notebooks/Untitled.ipynb", "type": "log", "logger": "NotebookApp", "thread": "MainThread", "level": "WARNING", "module": "log", "line_no": 52}
```

But with the change and with JSON logging enabled, we get something like this:

```json
{"written_at": "2020-10-12T16:31:16.698Z", "written_ts": 1602520276698143000, "msg": "", "type": "log", "logger": "NotebookApp", "thread": "MainThread", "level": "WARNING", "module": "log", "line_no": 50, "status": 404, "method": "GET", "ip": "127.0.0.1", "uri": "/nbextensions/widgets/notebook/js/extension.js", "request_time": 7.66, "referer": "http://localhost:8888/notebooks/Untitled.ipynb"}
```

In the former, note that the request fields are in the `msg` field, but in the latter the `ns` request fields are split out as specific fields in the log record which is the goal (for me anyway) so you can build queries in a distributed logging system like ELK or LogDNA and filter on things like `request_time:>=30`.

The numbers you have presented are correct. That is the actual cost history of the swarm over the iterations. But `ftol` does not deal with the actual costs. The cost based early stopping is not yet implemented. `ftol` is a relative measure.

The stopping criterion is defined by the improvement on the `best_cost_yet_found`. So if the change is big enough the iterations continue.

```py
# print(i, self.swarm.best_cost, best_cost_yet_found, delta)
22 -56.0 -56.0 True
23 -56.0 -56.0 True
24 -56.0 -56.0 True
25 -58.0 -56.0 False  # <- Here the change is big enough to let the algorithm continue
26 -58.0 -58.0 True
27 -58.001 -58.0 True  # <- Here there is a small improvement (not big enough for delta to change)
                       # This is the exact case where we still want to consider the iterations for
                       # early_stopping, but on the other hand the algorithm has not stalled.
28 -58.001 -58.001 True
29 -58.001 -58.001 True
```
Again this implementation is early_stopping based and not based on a cutoff threshold (as that will change for each optimization problem). If you're having problems running the tests cases, please try running through the logic with some sample values in a list.

No, but this is an existing API that we would change if we didn't carefully introduced this new flag. The user has to explicitly set this flag to True to agree to the API change. Otherwise, we would break code for existing users of our TFPolicy API.
I think this is a typical path we should go whenever we change an API:
1) Flag explicitly set to allow for the new API to be used.
2) Set this flag to True by default, catch possible errors for those users that are still on the old API, and warn if flag is left False.
3) Error if flag is left to False.
4) Remove flag and all checks for it in the code: Behave always as if flag was set to True.

The `Union` typing is only in a test file that parameterizes a test to test both old and new modes and here the difference is stark. A `Pex` has a digest and a path, A `VenvPex` has a digest, 2 Scripts and a dict of optional other asked-for Scripts. They;re totally different.

In MSSQL `db_1` is the database and `schema_1` is the schema. Postgres doesn't have the concept of multiple databases on the same server, so the whole thing `db_1.schema_1` is just the name of the schema. This will probably confuse anyone reading this. It will get even more confusing when creating postgres integration tests, and we initialize schems with names `db_1.schema_1`.

It might be worth just not specifying `db_1` in the `postgresql_schema_info`. Simply have `schema_1` as the schema name. MSSQL and Postgres are two different databases, and our schema setups for both of them don't have to be identical. In fact they might further diverge as we start testing dialect-specific features, like array types, or inheritance.

I went looking for how tensorflow and pytorch do it.

Tensorflow has a bidirectional wrapper in the Keras module (https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/keras/layers/Bidirectional) which is strictly batch-major and produces a default shape `[batch, len, num_dir * hidden]`. They have a `merge_mode` parameter (default concat) that can combine the two directions directly by e.g. summing them thus, producing `[batch, len, hidden]`. This is also what we do at Wolfram. They also have `merge_mode = None` which produces a list of two tensors of shape `[batch, len, hidden]`, one for the forward and one for the backward. Thus Tensorflow doesn't seem to have a `num_directions` at all.

Pytorch (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.LSTM.html) is time-major by default but can switch to batch-major. The two directions are concatenated by default, thus producing `[len, batch, num_dir * hidden]` (or `[batch, len, num_dir * hidden]`). They also have an "unpacked" mode which produces `[len, batch, hidden, num_dir]` (or `[batch, len, hidden, num_dir]`).

So they are all treat `num_dir` pretty differently from ONNX.

> From reading, it seems like you are generating multiple conceptual resolves which are each independently usable, and then selecting from them for a particular consumer.

Locks aside - this is fundamentally what a PEX is. It contains multiple resolves and selects the correct one at runtime. A lock file for a Pex then has to do the same thing.

> Does that mean there is never an exact match for the consumer?

No, although it's likely you don't have the correct notion of what exact match means. For a lock generated for --platform you can have an exact match if you export for the same --platform. And that's about it. Otherwise, the lock was generated for an interpreter and the only way to be sure the match is exact in that case is if the interpreter is the exact same interpreter, which means the exact same ordered set of supported tags and the exact same environment marker environment dict.

That aside and accepting the vague notion of exact match, if you did only support exact matches, then you could never support LockStyle.SOURCES or the forthcoming LockStyle.CROSS_PLATFORM. Even those aside, with a LockStyle.STRICT for a set of requirements that encloses a - say - universal wheel set - you'd not want to fail an export just because the target being used on this machine doesn't match the one on the machine used to generate the lock in the 1st place.

This is not regression, 'fillna` does not consider `as_index` parameter at all, so to bypass our `as_index=False` logic that incorrectly processes `fillna` index, it's better set `as_index=True`.

Why we haven't seen this problem before? At 1.2.0 version pandas fixed a bug, where `dropna=True` parameter doesn't drop `nan` groups.
```python
import pandas
import numpy as np

data = {"a": [np.nan, 2, np.nan, 2], "b": [1, 2, 3, np.nan]}
df = pandas.DataFrame(data)
res1 = df.groupby("a", as_index=False).fillna(method="ffill")
res2 = df.groupby("a", as_index=True).fillna(method="ffill")
assert res1.equals(res2)
print(res1)

# pandas 1.1.5 output:
#      b
# 0  NaN
# 1  2.0
# 2  NaN
# 3  2.0

# pandas 1.2.0+ output
#      b
# 1  2.0
# 3  2.0
```
The latest pandas version processes the` dropna=True` parameter correctly and drops `nan` groups, so the index is not monotonic range anymore (as our logic considers it when `as_index=False`).

I don't think it's necessary to look into it too deeply. The only thing I'm curious about is why we create the default group at all. But arguably I'm in a better position to find out why than you are, since I know the people that wrote that code originally :-)

It occurred to me that an unfortunate effect of using C++ thread_local is that every single pybind11 extension module will add an additional TLS variable --- that could make the thread-local storage rather large, as there can easily be a large number of extension modules.

To avoid that, the Python TLS API could be used instead --- the key that is allocated would have to be stored in the internals struct (which would make the ABI incompatible), or if we really want to maintain ABI compatibility, could be accessed via a separate PyCapsule that is handled in the same way as the existing internals struct.  Probably it would make more sense to just break the ABI, and take the opportunity to merge in the other ABI-breaking changes, though I don't have sufficient context to really offer much judgement on that decision.

That result seems to be from the interpreted `toolz` version of frequencies. The compiled cytools version is >2x faster than the `.count` version. The count version need to iterate over `set(terms)` rather than term which slows it down more, making it closer to 3x slower than compiled cytoolz.
Counter is around the same speed as the interpreted version of `frequencies`.

```python
%timeit for _ in toolz.frequencies(term).items(): pass
    1.16 µs ± 23.5 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit for _ in ((v, term.count(v)) for v in term): pass
    826 ns ± 46.9 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit for _ in cytoolz.frequencies(term).items(): pass
    347 ns ± 10.5 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit for _ in ((v, term.count(v)) for v in set(term)): pass
    1.01 µs ± 49.5 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit for _ in ((v, count) for v, count in Counter(term).items()): pass
    1.46 µs ± 15.2 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)

%timeit for _ in Counter(term).items(): pass
    1.08 µs ± 20.2 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000000 loops each)
```

Correct, it gets called over and over to determine whether there is some eval duration left (>0) to run.

For `evaluation_duration_unit=episodes` (and auto duration!):
- Run n more episodes: Each worker will - on a call to its `sample()` run exactly one episode (as this is configured via the worker's batch_mode=complete_episodes and rollout_fragment_length=1 settings).
- Then check again, whether training is done and we can stop

For `evaluation_duration_unit=timesteps` (and auto duration!):
- Run [num-workers * fragment-len * num_envs_per_worker] more timesteps: note that this is the minimum that one `sample()` on all workers will do.

In the non-auto case:
The result of the `duration_fn` is used to determine, how many more `sample()` (on which eval workers) we need to call. This is important to reach the exact desired number of timesteps/episodes with n workers.

Thanks for the logs! That explains it. The key lines are these reads of the `BASE` register at offset 0xf8 from the first two APs 0 and 1: ``` 0005506:DEBUG:dap:read_ap:000024 (addr=0x000000f8) -> 0xe00ff002 ``` ``` 0005521:DEBUG:dap:read_ap:000039 (addr=0x010000f8) -> 0xe00ff002 ``` This is the layout of the MEM-AP `BASE` register (from Arm ADI v5.2 specification, debug_interface_v5_2_architecture_specification_IHI0031F.pdf, §C2.6.1, page C2-172): The important parts of the returned value 0xe00ff002 is the two least significant bits = 0x2: - Format, bit[1]: 0x1, meaning the value is the current (non-deprecated) base register format. All good. - P, bit[0]: 0x0, Not set! _**This is the problem. P=0 means that there is no ROM table.**_ From the same ADI v5.2 specification: So, pyocd isn't doing anything wrong. Somehow the chip has an incorrectly configured `BASE` register. One question is why the J-Link continues to read the ROM table even though the P bit is cleared to say "there is no ROM table here". Maybe because it sees that the remainder of the `BASE` value is non-zero? Does the J-Link have specific support for the YTM32B1MD0? If so, it's probably just using a workaround they added. ---- Now the question is how to best work around this. It is important for pyocd to parse the ROM table, so that all the correct CoreSight components are detects. For instance, breakpoints and watchpoints will not work if the FPB and DWT components are not detected in the ROM table and hooked up to the core. I'll think about this and reply shortly with a proposed method to work around the invalid P bit.

The idea behind current model is that `SubMac` acts like a "more capable" radio. It can be in a single state at a time and it provides a bunch of APIs to next layers (`Mac`) to control it and put it in different states: 
- `Send()` to enter tx
- `Receive()` to enter rx mode
- `Sleep()` to go to sleep mode
- We also have a fancier `CslSample()` which puts `SubMc` in CSL sampling state (which it then does CSL sampling based on period/channel/etc).

As a model-isv, Salesforce.org sets the standard for on-platform development processes. Persistent orgs (sandboxes) have state drift over time. Multiple dev working on multiple sandboxes eventually become out of sync, tests become flakey, and people begin to lose confidence that what was developed in a sandbox will work the same in a production environment. @davidmreed has coined this "The Org Problem".

Scratch orgs (along with the CumulusCI flow; the workflow we recommend using which has a section unto itself elsewhere in the docs) allow for things to be done in a repeatable fashion across all members of a development team. With a persistent org, "starting fresh" (the equivalent of spinning up a new scratch org, installing a package and its dependencies along with any unpackaged metadata) is much more complicated.

I'm ok with just removing mention of the ability to connect to persistent orgs. We specific docs on how to connect persistent orgs which people can find if this is something they want to pursue. We just won't explicitly layout how to use the "CumulusCI Flow" with persistent orgs in our docs.

> @stuhood Unless I'm missing something, this would require the user to enumerate the sources from the input file, and would not allow the user to supply a glob, because the files matched by a glob wouldn't be known until after the input target has produced its outputs (potentially after the process has run).

Have thought about this some more, and you're right that "knowing the list of files that will be generated" is an important distinction in this case... but not the only one. The actual content of the files is also important for dependency inference -- but due to cost concerns (to infer dependencies for most languages, you'd have to generate all code in the repository), codegen doesn't currently run as part of dependency inference.

----

Currently, code generation (`GenerateSourcesRequest`):
* Does not need to know ahead of time which files it will generate
* Does not run before/as-part-of `Dependencies` calculations (because dependency inference implementations avoid calling it)
    * Instead, if you want dependencies inferred for codegen'd targets, you need to add dependency inference for the `gen+lang` pair: e.g. `thrift+python`.
    * That means that, as written, this PR will not have dependency inference applied. 

Meanwhile, target generation (`TargetGenerator`, and in particular `TargetFilesGenerator`):
* Does need to ["know" ahead of time](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/851998457bb87d2397c9f041fa372fbbd97f2473/src/python/pants/engine/internals/graph.py#L1420-L1447) which files/targets it will generate
    * The "know" here mainly refers to the fact that `TargetFilesGenerator` uses a relatively light operation to list files (`SourcesPaths`) rather than snapshotting them.
* Does run before `Dependencies` calculations
* Participates in dependency inference by actually capturing the files.
    * In the case of `TargetFilesGenerator` this is snapshotting files off of disk.

----

I think that users generating code using `experimental_shell_command` (or via `http_source`) would expect dependency inference to be applied (unlike with "native" `GenerateSourcesRequest` implementations, where there is an expectation that the `@rule` author implements inference in a generation specific way). And as mentioned above, I think that generic codegen is probably too expensive to run as part of dependencies calculations.

So to refine my suggestion: hooking this in to `TargetFilesGenerator`, using either codegen (if it doesn't make the graph too complex: unclear), or a new more limited `@union` seems like a path forward.

This hack for hiding the TTYs is necessary because we rely on some extremely stupid bash mechanisms to setup our env. Specifically, we want `.bashrc` to execute, which will only happen in interactive mode, AND if you source it manually it will immediately cancel itself. This means we HAVE to start in full-on interactive mode (which we do by launching a login session for some reason???????) including bash and SSH settings (see default SSH flags). BUT this causes apps to print an extreme amount of progress bars, "\r"-based stuff, control codes, etc. This was fine when we just dumped them to a local TTY, but now that we both try to parse the output and write it to file, we end up with `\ESC` gibberish and literally hundreds of `13% |====>-------| (5MB/s)` lines from pip downloading packages.

So, for backwards-compatibility reasons (not changing the env), we keep the interactive session, but we kill the tty-detection in the command by piping everything.

If this seems like an extremely weird way to run ssh commands, then yeah no kidding, hence the workaround is also extremely weird. The interactive session hack also causes the bash "job control is not available" message in the logs (cause login sessions not started by the `login` command are actually all subtly invalid and broken).

P.S. I actually suspect that we depend on a Deep-Learning-AMI-specific file to execute and not `.bashrc`, though I've only been told about `.bashrc`; anyway this does not matter because we still need to preserve backwards compatibility

P.P.S. I think the part of the DLAMI file that we need does some extremely hacky and inappropriate conda setup but we nevertheless depend on it and there seems to be no clean workaround

All imports from the `globus_cli.services` subpackage are deferred as a performance optimization.
I do it by policy because it's easier to be consistent than to reason about which _particular_ imports cause perf regressions.

In the case of `globus_cli.services.transfer`, we have a custom subclass of `globus_sdk.TransferClient`. Subclassing forces the `globus_sdk` lazy importer to pull in `TransferClient` -- this turns into a dependency chain which pulls in `requests` and causes a big performance hit for tab completions and `--help`.
I prefer to do this by setting `__all__`.

Hmm so is there a way we can guarantee that once java gc happens, `ObjectRef`s that aren't in scope anymore will get deleted?

There's a similar problem in Python where the GC doesn't collect some refs due to cycles, and the `ObjectRef` itself is small so having a lot of unreachable `ObjectRef`s doesn't create enough memory pressure to trigger a full GC sweep. So when we hit an OOM, we trigger a full GC sweep on all Python workers to try and collect any `ObjectRef`s ([here](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/ce3f5427394af288f9699bed3d479c47181b252f/python/ray/_raylet.pyx#L581) is the GC callback that we give to the C++ worker to call). Is there an equivalent in Java that we should add?

As for the snippet inclusion, there's a few points.
1) doctests exist and they are testable. It's a well-known standard, use it. https://docs.python.org/3/library/doctest.html / https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/extensions/doctest.html. The runner for them is already added to the CI so just add those.
2) It is possible to include parts of files as code blocks in RST/Sphinx. You can use https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/directives.html#directive-literalinclude which also allows you to include the contents partially and add some highlighting. Use this in the actual documents.
3) Do not use `.. literalinclude` in the docstrings. It's unreadable, untestable, and unmaintainable. It's a very bad idea.

You can include huge bits of docs in docstrings but many projects have moved away from this practice because a lot of prose may make it harder to deal with actually coding stuff. Plus you'll probably be unable to create https://diataxis.fr structured site (which you should). This is why these approaches are most often combined.

Typical failure if `os.path.abspath` is this function:
https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/0a8005ec10cf16942a20ee9eec4ac7965a2ad8cc/certbot/tests/storage_test.py#L126-L136

The problem is that here, we have links defining a relative path target.

Let's split this up.

Supposing we are in path `C:\Tmp\dir1`. This method creates for instance, on path `C:\Tmp\dir1\live\test\cert.pem` a link pointing to the relative path `..\..\archive\test\cert1.pem`. This is a perfectly valid link, both on Windows and Linux. Target will be resolved relatively to the path of the link.

However, in `_apply_mode`, we receive the path `C:\Tmp\dir1\live\test\cert.pem`. Python sees that it is a link, and so call `os.readlink`. What `os.readlink` returns, is the target path registered in the link, so ... `..\..\archive\test\cert1.pem`. But if we call know `os.path.abspath` on this relative path, it will be resolved against the current work dir, that can be anything (like a call to `os.chdir` could have been done in the mean time). So the realpath may be incorrectly resolved to the wrong path.

It is the case here, `os.path.abspath` will return `C:\archive\test\cert1.pem`.

What you want, is to resolve the relative path against the absolute path of the link. This is what the condition under `if not os.path.isabs(file_path)`, obviously skipping this logic if the registered target path in the link is already absolute.

>>> import numpy

array([[5, 6],
       [7, 5]])
>>> x.flat = [10,11]
>>> x
array([[10, 11],
       [10, 11]])
```

`read_array.flat[:len(response.read_array)] = response.read_array` leaves the extra array elements unmodified when `len(read_array) > len(response.read_array)`:
```
>>> x.flat[:2] = [20,21]
>>> x
array([[20, 21],
       [10, 11]])
```

and it ignores the extra array elements when `len(read_array) < len(response.read_array)`:

It is an alternative of generic methods using `TypeVar` when the generic is a union of disjoint boundaries. Basically it allows to define several possible set of types for the method signature, both on the parameters types and the returned type.

Here it expresses the fact that the type of the third element in the returned tuple depends on the type of `chain_path`: `str` for a `str`, `Optional[str]` for an `Optional[str]`, because we know from the implementations that `chain_path` is returned as it in the third element of the tuple.

The two methods methods annotated with `@overload` define the signatures, then the actual implementation is given without annotation. Obviously the implementation can be typed and its boundary signature must be compatible with all signatures.

How I ended up here was:

1. I noticed that MIDI files have a tendency to produce simultaneities that don't collapse into chords, because of differing durations. So iterating elements and checking the element at i + 1 might still give you the same offset; I need the next distinct offset. I didn't want to have to look backwards in the elements array, so I realized I wanted sorted streams.
2. So I made MIDI parsing produce a stream with isSorted reported as True.
3. Then I went to add a test case to testQuantize, which builds streams with insert(). I then had to debug why the stream was reporting itself as unsorted. I chose between using ignoreSort=True versus doing an explicit sort.
4. ignoreSort=True wasn't preserving the value of isSorted (I agree that feels like a should-be-core thing, though)

1. User neither passed `multichannel` nor `channel_axis` and relied on automatic detection of color channel for `(M, N, 3)`. Since v0.19 we silently broke his workflow. This patch recovers the original behavior for one release and notifies the user.

3. User read the misleading RuntimeWarning, and nevertheless started using `channel_axis=None` for a grayscale image. The warning continued. In case the shape was `(M, N, 3)` we broke his code otherwise he was fine. This patch recovers the original behavior but does not notify him.

```
Module 'KNN' has no attribute 'keys_index' (This attribute exists on the Python module, but we failed to convert Python type: 'faiss.swigfaiss.IndexFlat' to a TorchScript type. Only tensors and (possibly nested) tuples of tensors, lists, or dictsare supported as inputs or outputs of traced functions, but instead got value of type IndexFlat..
```

Ah, right, good catch! UUID1 is local timestamp based, and the resolution isn't good enough to guarantee uniqueness at this level.

I think of UUID4 as "slower", but it's so marginal that you need a very large number of calls to notice.

Just because I think it's interesting...
<details><summary>UUID v1 vs v4 vs v5 timing</summary>

This is what I ran, expecting v5 to be significantly faster. I was surprised to see that it's the slowest by far.
```
$ python -m timeit 'import uuid; namespace = uuid.uuid4(); x = [uuid.uuid5(namespace, "foo") for _ in range(100)]'
500 loops, best of 5: 496 usec per loop
$ python -m timeit 'import uuid; x = [uuid.uuid4() for _ in range(100)]'
500 loops, best of 5: 377 usec per loop
$ python -m timeit 'import uuid; x = [uuid.uuid1() for _ in range(100)]'
1000 loops, best of 5: 302 usec per loop
```

So our speedups are...

|      | v1      | v4      | v5    |
|-----|---------|---------|---------|
| v1 | 100% | 20%   | 39%  |
| v4 |  -        | 100% | 24%  |
| v5 |  -        | -         | 100% |


Of course, the scale here is microseconds! So it doesn't really matter. 😁 

</details>

> Is there a better place for these `QueryRule`'s?

For now, the place that I linked to in my comment above (in `src/python/pants/init/engine_initializer.py`). Formalizing that would be good, but it's something that only _non_-plugins are actually able to do (since a plugin cannot run queries: only install `@rules` that will satisfy queries).

> And can the `bsp` goal add these `QueryRule`'s somehow to the scheduler only when the `bsp` goal is invoked?

No. The rule graph is compiled once when the scheduler is created (generally: when `pantsd` starts up), so they need to be known ahead of time.
